title: Debugging, Review, and Adversarial Prompting · Failure Case: Adversarial Cases
summary: Diagnose a failing build and review the repair against a base branch. Planned artifact: Add and verify the prompt adversarial cases increment in an evidence-first debug and review playbook. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Test prompts and code against misleading context, malformed inputs, and instruction injection.
Define review baseline, risk categories, and evidence required for actionable findings.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: Predict Adversarial Cases
steps.instruction:
Use a changed theory case derived from this module: prompt debug review. Preserve inputs, constraints, an observable trace, and a boundary result.

Use a changed theory case derived from this module: prompt debug review. Preserve inputs, constraints, an observable trace, and a boundary result. Before reading, predict which action preserves the Adversarial Cases contract.

content.text: Test prompts and code against misleading context, malformed inputs, and instruction injection. Use this as an observable contract: name its preconditions, trace the state or value change, and state what evidence would disprove the result.

content.text: Compare plausible approaches against the decision, constraints, evidence quality, and cost of being wrong.

content.title: Worked case pattern

steps.title: Build Adversarial Cases evidence
steps.instruction: Append a contract headed "Evidence: prompt-adversarial-cases-pyzk81" with concrete Goal, Context, Constraints, Output, and Done when sections tailored to this case.
steps.why: Building a runnable or mechanically reviewable artifact turns Adversarial Cases into observable performance evidence.

content.code:

Constraints: [replace with authority and safety boundaries]
Output: [replace with the artifact and evidence shape]
Done when: [replace with changed-case acceptance criteria]

steps.title: Defend the Adversarial Cases model
steps.instruction: Write a concise evidence note that explains when Adversarial Cases applies, what can fail, and how the changed case was verified.
steps.why: Retrieval and explanation expose gaps that recognition questions can hide.

content.title: Evidence defense
